segment-text: EXT. CHICAGO STREETS - DAWN

Ah, it's Fall. Not in a cozy, peaceful way. In that slushy miserable Midwest way. Strong winds bend the trees of Lakeshore Drive. Grey rain drips from the Magnificent Mile storefronts to the street signs of the suburbs.
EXT. PORTOKALOS HOUSE - EARLY MORNING
The lights turn on at the Portokalos house.
EXT. PORTOKALOS HOUSE - DAY, CONTINUOUS

A large old black Cadillac backs down the driveway of the Corinthian-columned home. It drives at a wrong angle. Stops. Drives forward, corrects. Backs out again. Corrects again. Backs out. The car drives ten feet and stops at the very next home. SFX: HONKING. That house door opens.
I/E. CAR/MILLER HOUSE - DAY, CONTINUOUS
CLOSE-UP: A smudged "HAPPINESS IS BEING GREEK" Greek flag cover on an OPA! license plate. PAN UP....
INT. GUS'S CADILLAC/MILLER HOUSE - DAY, CONTINUOUS

SFX: Car door closing. Through the windshield, in the passenger seat, TOULA PORTOKALOS, 40's, is burrowed in a scarf, her glasses fog with every breath. GUS PORTOKALOS, 70's, looks to his right, concerned. about to put the car in gear, but then: He is
You better get married, you're starting to look old.
Startled, Toula looks up, realizes her dad is not talking to her. In fact, he is looking in the rear-view mirror, at the back seat. At Toula's daughter. Dad!!
You can't say that to her!
PARIS, Gus's 17 year old granddaughter, pops forward, looks at him with horror and dismay. Pappou!!
PARIS
Gus shrugs, puts the car in gear. Toula, not looking together this morning, or ever, shakes her head.
Well, that's a family tradition. My dad used to say that to me and now he just said it to my daughter. She's 17.
EXT. MILLER HOUSE - DAY, CONTINUOUS WIDE SHOT: The car, in front of the modest Miller home, which
is beside the Portokalos home, now drives....
My family worries about each other. We're close. Extremely close.
.... ten feet, stops at the very next house. This house is decorated with all things Greek, complete with a Parthenon Marbles door frame. Toula's brother, NICK PORTOKALOS, 40, waves from the window.
We see no difference between hugging and suffocation.
Gus honks. (Annoyed every morning, NEIGHBORS glare out
I/E. GUS'S CADILLAC/NICK'S HOUSE - DAY, CONTINUOUS
Gus wags a finger.
Toula, find your daughter a Greek boyfriend before she does what you did.
What the -GUS Married a Xeno -TOULA My husband -GUS Not Greek, a Xeno -TOULA
How can you say that-Paris leans forward, angry at her mother.
PARIS
Chagrined, Toula turns and looks out the windshield.
Do I speak for all mothers of teens when I ask, does it ever get better?
Gus lowers Toula's window, cold wind and leaves hit her in the face.
EXT. NICK'S HOUSE - DAY, CONTINUOUS
Nick's wife, ILARIA, 30's, pregnant, waves as her TWIN BOYS, 4, pull MARIA PORTOKALOS, 70's, holding the "leashes" of their harness backpacks.
(yells) Gus, I put your pills by your coffee.
Yeah, yeah...
Nick, a parka over a robe, runs out with COSTA, 9.
I/E. GUS'S CADILLAC/NICK'S HOUSE - CONTINUOUS
Gus is always thrilled at the sight of his son. Nicko!
Nick leans in the passenger window, holds up Costa's project on, of course, Greece.
Hey, hey. Dad, pop the trunk, I'm freezing my 'nads off.
Costa climbs over Paris, squishes too close, wags a finger like an old Greek man.
Too much makeup.
Toula sees Paris is annoyed being schooled by a 'tween.
Costa, did you finish drawing Alexander the Great?
(proudly) Uh huh. And I sculpted a Parthenon out of soap.
Costa holds up a crudely carved blob. And a typed page.
Oh no, spell-check corrected Spanakopita to Spina Bifida.
Gus lowers the back window, Nick leans in to kiss Costa, who giggles and kisses him back.
Ah, kisses from your child. That's over.
Impatient to go, Gus points to a house, and drives off.
EXT. ATHENA'S HOUSE/STREET - DAY, CONTINUOUS
A Greek flag waves from a post, and a la the islands, the house is whitewashed with a bright blue dome roof. Holding her BABY SON, ATHENA, late 40's, and her husband YIANNI, late 40's, appear.
My sister did it right. Just as her sons became possessed by the teen wolf hormones, she'd just pop out another one.
Gus honks hello. Athena yells back into the house.
Move it, Aristotle!!
Athena hands the baby to Yianni, steps out on a Mykonosshaped welcome mat -- it plays the Greek National Anthem. ARISTOTLE, 9, runs out and by Athena.
(yells) Put on your hat!
I/E. GUS'S CADILLAC/ATHENA'S HOUSE - CONTINUOUS
Aristotle puts on his hat, crawls over Paris, squishes between her and Costa.
Paris, come by the restaurant some time.
You should butter the garlic bread.
Can't wait.
(not getting it) Yeah, it's fun.
Athena licks her thumb, wipes food off Aristotle's cheek. Then tries to do the same to Paris's mascara.
Toula, you seen these eyes? and Ian better fix that. (nope) Yep. Okay bye!
Gus drives as Athena yells out.
After school you have hockey! Your brothers are coming so make sure you score!
Gus drives off as Aristotle blows kisses to his mom.
You'll score, the Greeks invented hockey.
Paris eye rolls in the back, Toula eye rolls in the front. But Costa and Aristotle vehemently nod.
Yes, because what do you play hockey on? Ice.
What is the Greek word for ice. Payo.
Payo, puck, there you go.
There you go.
There you go....

Paris and Toula scowl out the windows of the car. The NOSY/CRANKY NEIGHBOR, MRS. WHITE, 60's, walks her dog and grimaces as the Cadillac passes her. Gus good-naturedly honks hello. Mrs. White jumps, then swears under her breath.
EXT. CHICAGO SUBURBS - DAY
Gus's Cadillac drives.
Now give me a word, any word and I will show you how the root of that word is Greek-Facebook!
EXT. HIGH SCHOOL STREET/STEPS - DAY
The Greeks invented Facebook. We called it the telephone.
Gus's car pulls up. The boys head to the lower school. Toula watches Paris get out. Of course, Gus has one more nugget of wisdom:
Paris, keep your eyeballs open now for a nice Greek boy. Make babies, don't waste your eggs.
Toula winces. Paris can barely contain her revulsion.
Outstanding.
Paris turns to the high school. Suddenly, Toula leaps from the car, grabs Paris.

(blurts, loud and fast) Paris, Pappou didn't mean to say anything hurtful, you're beautiful, so beautiful, of course you don't look old, he just says stuff like that, to, well, to me too, you don't need a boyfriend, you don't
have to get married or have babiesParis side-eyes the ever-present school loudmouth TOMMY, and the COOL STUDENTS, including TIAHN, CLIFFORD, all 17. And, BENNETT, 17.
Toula doesn't see the effect he has on Paris. Paris turns to glare at Toula.
(teeth gritted, sotto) Mother.
Toula stops talking, stares at her daughter.
When did my name change from-CUT TO:
EXT. MILLER DRIVEWAY - SUMMER DAY (FLASHBACK)
PARIS, 4, runs TO CAMERA: Mommy!!!
PARIS (AT FOUR) EXT. HIGH SCHOOL PARKING LOT - DAY (THE PRESENT)
(furious) Mother!!
Toula realizes she is holding Paris's face. hands. She drops her Paris turns, walks past GROUPS OF TEENS. She is alone.
Ooh, drama.
Toula blinks, looks up at: A banner hangs: COLLEGE FAIR TONIGHT.
EXT. CHICAGO STREETS - DAY The downtown Loop in the distance, the Cadillac drives
through Greek Town.
I/E. GUS'S CADILLAC/CHICAGO STREETS (THE PRESENT)
Gus drives, as Toula thinks.
I guess when my daughter started to pull away, I should have stepped back.
INT. SCHOOL GYM - DAY (FLASHBACK)
PACKED volleyball game. Paris is a Line Judge. Wearing a PARENT VOLUNTEER sticker, Toula works the SNACKS TABLE on the side.
But I stayed too close. I kept volunteering at her school. I wanted her to think I was cool again.

Toula keeps waving to Paris, who wishes she would go away. Then, a volleyball smacks Toula in the head. She drops. The GYM of PEOPLE sucks in air. Paris cringes at the attention. Toula is on the ground.
Then I remembered I've never been cool.

MARGE and EDIE, 40's, the overbearing Volunteer Moms, smirk into their hands. STUDENTS, including Tommy, are quietly giggling. ThenTOMMY (O.C.) Here comes the principal. The principal is backlit. It's IAN MILLER, 40's, and even though he looks worn down by the teen years, he is handsome.
My husband has always been cool. He just has it. And he always knows what to say.
Ian leans in.
You okay, babe?
Toula sits up, nods, sees: Paris looks at her with disdain.
I/E. GUS'S CADILLAC/CHICAGO STREETS - DAY (THE PRESENT)
Remembering this, Toula winces.
Just when my daughter doesn't want me around anymore, my parents need me more than ever.
Gus, driving, nods off. Toula nudges his chin, he wakes up. Toula sees they pass the SENIORS' REHAB CENTER.
INT. REHAB CENTER WAITING ROOM - ANOTHER DAY (FLASHBACK)
So I take them to get groceries, to
their doctors, and to Physical
Toula waits: through the glass, Gus does hip therapy. Toula blows off dust from a peanuts bowl. Eats some. The receptionist, DOMINIQUE, looks up.
The old guys suck the chocolate off those nuts.
Toula spits nuts.
I/E. GUS'S CADILLAC/OLYMPIC TRAVEL - DAY (THE PRESENT)
Gus drives past closed-down buildings, one with two tattered signs: OLYMPIC DRY CLEANING, and OLYMPIC TRAVEL AGENCY. Toula sees the sign.
I used to love being a travel agent. But in a tight economy the first things to go are luxuries like travel and dry cleaning.
I/E. GUS'S CADILLAC/DANCING ZORBA'S - DAY
The car pulls up to the family restaurant, DANCING ZORBA'S.
Luckily, people still eat.

Toula gets out, then helps her dad as he slowly gets himself out. Toula pulls a walking cane from the car. Gus looks around to be sure no one sees this, reluctantly takes it. Toula holds his arm as they walk. Gus slips, bumps into Toula, knocking off her glasses. Gus steps, accidentally crunching them to bits.
INT. DANCING ZORBA'S - DAY
Toula now squints, flips on the lights, hangs up their coats. Gus, exhausted already, limps to the corner booth. Toula hangs a worn sign in the window: WE NOW CATER, TOO!
My sister and brother have small children, so I help at the restaurant. It's what nice Greek girls do.
Toula puts on a mud-brown Seating Hostess jacket. Toula walks past a pie case filled with mini-bundt cakes, each with a Fall-colored orange or cranberry gerbera in its center.
Families that are close like mine, we make it through bad economies, wars and sickness because we stick together...

Toula goes to a drawer, finds a strip of empty disposable contact lenses. She remembers something, and rummages to find a pair of glasses. They're ugly and they were once hers. She puts them on. Toula looks as downcast and frumpy as she once did.
... but some of us just get stuck.
Toula looks out the window. Cars and PEOPLE pass her by. The day is as bleak as her mood.
INT. HIGH SCHOOL HALLWAY - DAY
In the exact same stance as her mother Toula, Paris stands, looking out the window. SMALL GROUPS OF STUDENTS huddle at lockers. She turns and heads into: Paris is alone.
INT. HIGH SCHOOL LIBRARY - DAY
Paris takes a book from a bookshelf -- Ian walks up.
Hey Paris --
Do not talk to me. I'm a narc.
People think
I -- shouldn't you be in class?
You track me all day! Mom is needy, Pappou wants to marry me off, Yiayia constantly tells me to never, ever let a boy touch my poulaki because once he feels it, he wants it. Ugh, this family!
STUDENTS look over at the voices from the bookshelf.
Save it for the therapist's couch. Riiiiiight?
He goes for a high five which no one returns.
Paris, your mom once felt the same way about being Greek-PARIS Dad, that's obtuse, why would I have an issue being Greek?
Paris points behind her to 5 cheerful posters declaring
upcoming meetings: Syrian Club!, Korean Club, Greek Club!,
Gay & Lesbian Club!, Handi-Capable Club!
I can't take that everyone is always in my business! Give me some air. I'm not a kid, I can be late to a class, there won't be a spasmodic catastrophic ripple in the space-time continuum. And yes,
four of those words are Greek. Paris storms away, Ian looks worn out.
EXT. HIGH SCHOOL - NIGHT BANNER: COLLEGE FAIR TONIGHT
PARENTS and STUDENTS enter the gym.
INT. HIGH SCHOOL GYM - NIGHT

BOOTHS line the walls. Fresh-faced REPS, from colleges from Harvard to Phoenix University, talk to STUDENTS and PARENTS. Slapping on a PARENT VOLUNTEER sticker, Toula looks for Ian, sees those overbearing moms, Marge and Edie, and ducks back. They intercept her. Hey, hi!
Marge and Edie click their pens, tap their clipboards.
Where were you when we set up?
Sorry, my pipes froze -EDIE And decorated the gym -TOULA
I had to plunge the sink -They stare her down, clicking their pens.
You're on Clean-Up.
Marge hands Toula a garbage can, they leave as Ian walks up. Hi.
Having been married 17 years, they barely look at each other. Hi.
Ian takes the garbage can from Toula, shakes his head.
You can say no to them.
Toula laughs ruefully.
Babe, it's been a long time since those two made fun of your lunch -Toula winces, remembering.
They called it moose-caca. Ian nods, he's heard it before.
Toula sees and points to Paris:
There's Paris ....
Paris moves to the next booth: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY.
Hey, if she goes to Northwestern, she'll stay in Chicago!
Please, oh please. over there? Nuh uh. Should we go
Ian gently pulls Toula back. Right.
There she is!
Toula's face freezes.
Did she just hear her mother?

THE PORTOKALOS FAMILY (Gus, Maria, UNCLE TAKI, ANGELO, NIKKI, her HUSBAND and TWO SONS, 20's, Nick, Costa, Athena, Aristotle in a pee-wee hockey jersey carried on the shoulders of FOUR BROTHERS, ages 18 to 27, plus SEVERAL MALE COUSINS) comes thundering down the aisle.
Did you invite the family?!
Toula gasps, realizes she made a mistake.
I told my mom.
Maria pounces on horrified Paris.
Be a dental hygienist! A mother working two days a week, perfect.
Nikki pulls a COMB from her Hairdressers's work smock.
Paris, be a hairdresser like me.
As the family gives their opinions to Paris, Gus nudges Ian, points to all the BOYS in the family.
Ian, look, everyone has boys. one girl. Vegetarian. Slow sperms. You,
Maria's cell rings.
It's Thea Voula on The Facetimes.
AUNT VOULA, 60's, appears on Maria's iPhone.
AUNT VOULA (ON MARIA'S CELL)
Now Gus and Uncle Taki are on a bench.
We need to find Paris a boyfriend.
How about Ariana Skoufis's boy?
Everyone from that island has six toes.
Let's wait until summer, we'll check his feet.
Aunt Voula comes in still talking into her iPad.
I don't see you, I don't see you. I see you.
She hangs up, greets Maria, shows her iPad.
Look at this App, 10,000 steps, I met my goal. Where's Taki? He
never answers his phone.
(wryly) Why stand when you can sit?
Maria points to Gus and Uncle Taki on the bench. Gus and Uncle Taki see their wives looking over at them.
Look tired so they'll leave us alone.
Gus and Uncle Taki slump. AT THE BOOTH:
Hey Angelo, you get my flatscreen TV?
Oh, I got it right here.
Angelo raps Nick in the groin, they laugh and wrestle. Costa grabs his cousin Aristotle and they do the same.
Alright, alright, break it up.
They ALL turn to see Ian's long time friend, MIKE, 40's.
Mike! Hey buddy! at this place? You miss working
Mike indicates the school and STUDENTS, shudders to Ian. No.
Too scary for me.
Mike wears a CHICAGO POLICE OFFICER uniform.
-- we picked up Mana-Yiayia. where is she?
They all look around for Mana-Yiayia. Mike lifts a table covering to find: Gus's mother, MANAYIAYIA, 90's. She holds a Tupperware of: MANA-YIAYIA
The family descends on Mana-Yiayia and eats. Aunt Voula hands her iPad to the Northwestern Rep. crowd around Paris. The women
Take a picture.
Nikki moves beside Athena.
Pull my neck back.
Yeah, do mine.

The women put their hands on the back of each other's necks, pulling back each other's neck skin so their faces look younger. Paris is horrified. Toula sees Paris watch STUDENTS walk around her giant family.
Hey, Bennett. Bennett.
Paris turns. There he is. And he saw her family. Toula sees this, turns to the family.
We should go. Why?
Ian impatiently indicates the waiting Northwestern Rep.
So Paris can go to Northwestern.
That's right, she's going to Northwestern.
Paris scowls at that decision being made for her.
The family gasps. Gus and Taki stand.
The family murmurs. pull him aside:
Nick and Angelo quickly step to the Rep,
(sotto) If my niece wants to come to your school, you're going to say "welcome," got it?
(sotto) And tuition discount means a box of steaks for you.
The Rep looks uncomfortable.
None of this is actually up to me.
No one sees Paris slip away. Gus steps up.
She's only coming there if you teach Greek history.
Toula grimaces.
The Greeks invented Italy. Actually, no.
Yes. Nope.
No, sir.
I'm a Sephardic Jew.
Then you Greek. No, my family is Spanish-GUS Alexander the Great went through Spain spreading his seed. You Greek. Some of the family nods.
Some look doubtful. Toula cringes.
Gus, it's not the time. And it's ridiculous.
They all start arguing with the Rep and each other.
No one notices her.
PARIS (CONT'D)
Everyone turns around. Paris holds up four pamphlets.
Alabama, Florida, Texas, New York. These are the colleges I'm applying to!! Far, far away from here.
Everyone is stunned. Toula blurts:
Why do you want to leave me? Paris's eyes bug at that.
She stalks out. The Family is devastated by the thought of losing Paris. shakes his head to Toula.
Didn't I say, get your daughter a Greek boyfriend?
Gus turns back to the Rep.
And you, educate yourself! We are all descendants of Alexander the Great! I am for sure!
No, you're not! The family argues.
Toula and Ian look miserable. Nick and Angelo threaten the Rep. The Rep turns away. Mike shows his gun. To Mana-Yiayia, offering spanakopita.
EXT. DANCING ZORBA'S - DAY
Late February Thaw.
Give me a word, any word and I will show you how the root of that word is Greek!
INT. DANCING ZORBA'S - DAY
Okay, Chimichanga!
In a booth, Costa and Aristotle drink tea and play
Sure. Chimi is from the Greek word keema which means spicy beef and changa is from the Greek word tsanta which means purse. So, meat that is shaped like a purse: chimichanga. There you go!
There you go.
From the next booth, Gus doesn't look up from his newspaper.
There you go.
At the counter, Toula watches Paris buttering garlic bread. Nick and Angelo come in, mid-conversation.
Anyway, just meet her, she's from Holland.
Nah, I don't speak Hollandaise. Quit setting me up.

They see Athena and Nikki through the short order window, motion "hunger" at their mouths as they sit at the counter. Nikki and Athena place a plate of sandwiches at the window. Toula passes sandwiches to the men, puts one on a plate, gives it to Paris.
Paris doesn't look up. Toula indicates herself.
Um, just because you don't want this to be your job when you're my age, doesn't mean you have to run off to college in another city. I hope you applied to some local colleges too.
Paris looks Toula straight on.
Why do parents always say, "dream big", when they really mean, "but not too big." Like, "fly little birdie, oh wait, let me hold your wings." Toula cannot deny this is true.
Gus walks by, coughs into his hand.
What?
TOULA
Gus covers, jerks a thumb toward the back. Office.
Gus passes Mana-Yiayia running a floor-waxer bigger than her.
INT. DANCING ZORBA'S OFFICE - DAY, LATER
Toula comes in. As if he's hiding something, Gus quickly closes a cupboard, limps to his seat.
Dad, let me take you back to physical therapy.
I can fix it myself.
Gus waves her off, points to a chair, Toula sits.
Please don't lecture me about my daughter.
I'm going to find her a boyfriend, she'll stay in Chicago. Relax, you're getting wrinkles like the dried figs in my village.
Toula tries to relax her face. Gus motions to the computer.
Show me how to work that.
You want to learn how to use a computer?!
Yeah. What's that called where people search for... DNA?
Uh... a crime scene?
No, where they came from. Oh.
Gus nods.
The Find Your Ancestry site?
Toula types it in. Gus looks at the site.
Ha! I'm going to prove to your mother that I'm related to Alexander the Great. Don't tell her. He holds a finger to his lips, shhh, it's a secret.
No noooo. You'll have to type in your relatives all the way back to 300 BC. You get frustrated just adding up checks.
Your mother talks to me and I lose count! Show me.
Toula exhales. Then, points to the cursor.
Okay, this is called a mouse. Why?
I don't know. Put your hand on it. Gus covers it completely.
Gus does.
All right, now look on the screen.
Don't make up words, you confuse me.
Okay. See, on the scree -- uh, computer? That's your mouse moving.
Gus stares. Moves the mouse. face lights up. Then... sees it. His entire
I got this. A LITTLE LATER:
Gus pounds the table, Toula turns from the cabinet.
Where did it go?!
You have to press SAVE!
Toula tries to help Gus input information. Gus and Ian scream at the computer screen.
No quit!!
GUS
Gus is inputting numbers Ian is reciting. gets them all wrong. Of course, Gus Gus is inputting a document's numbers as Nick sleeps.
GUS (CONT'D)
Toula comes in with snacks. Nick wakes up, tries to shove Toula into the chair. Gus never turns around as Nick and Toula wrestle to force each other to stay.
ANOTHER NIGHT:

The desk lamp glows as Gus works alone. He puts a document into a dusty tin which once held Greek cookies... sees his marriage certificate, picks it up. CLOSE-UP: the signature line is empty. Gus is confused.
EXT. CHURCH - DAY

An OVERHEAD SHOT of the parking lot: Gus's Cadillac is parked askew with its rear tire up on the curb. The service is over, Gus and Maria walk out with Mana-Yiayia, Aunt Voula, Uncle Taki, Angelo, Athena, Yianni, Nick, Ilaria, their BABY BOY, Nikki, her husband, all the SONS. Toula, Ian and Paris come out. Maria indicates the FAMILY.
See Paris, you can't move away, who would you go to church with?
This lands on Toula and Ian, Paris doesn't have an answer. Gus points to a GROUP of GREEK BOYS.
Look Paris, husbands.
Paris quickly turns away.
I'll be in the car.
I'll go with you. Me too.
(dryly) Of course.
Aunt Voula nudges Angelo toward a group of ATTRACTIVE WOMEN.
Angelo, go pick a wife.
Let it go, Ma.
The PRIEST introduces ANNA, 40's.
Gus, Maria, we have a new family. Maria shakes Anna's hand.
How do you like Chicago?
We love it! Da Bears.
(not getting it) Okay.
We're the Mikoses. I'm Anna, that's
my husband, George-Gus sees the back of GEORGE, 40's, chatting to a GROUP.
I am Maria Portokalos this is my husband, Costa. Do you have children?
Do you have a son? We do!
Gus grunts to Uncle Taki.
Meat-eaters. Maria, invite them to the restaurant. Excuse me, I have to go talk to the Priest.
Gus heads to the Priest who is heading back up the stairs.
Go slow, your hip.
As Anna's husband George gets closer to them... Nikki brightens.
I know you!
(so proud) Ah, yes, you must recognize my husband George from the channel 7 news.
George flashes gorgeous white teeth.
Hello ladies.
Nikki gets excited.
Yeah, I know you! Do the thing.
"Good evening Chicagoland."
George winks. Nikki unzips her top, grins. Gus goes to the church entrance, holds out the certificate and a pen to the Priest.
Pater, look at this. Will you-- Never signed.
When he sees the unsigned line, the Priest looks alarmed.
INT. DANCING ZORBA'S - DAY
In the corner booth, Aunt Voula and Uncle Taki lean to Gus.
You're not married?!
Gus shushes them, looks around.
How can this be?
You know, the war! The village priest didn't finish seminary, there was no money for anything. I guess he just came back to town and performed the baptisms, funerals, the weddings, and no one knew!
Aunt Voula turns to Uncle Taki.
Lucky we got married in Athens. They cross themselves.
Uncle Taki picks up the paper -- no signature.
Our Priest just can't sign it?
Not allowed.
What did Maria say?
I don't know how to tell her, you know how upset she'll be.
INT. PORTOKALOS KITCHEN - DAY
Maria laughs.
We're not married?!
Maria holds the marriage certificate. WIDEN: Uncle Taki and Aunt Voula see that Toula, Ian, Nick, Ilaria, Athena and Yianni are stunned.
Maria laughs harder.
We were never married? children? Yes.
And we had
Maria loves it.
I'm a hippie!
Gus and Uncle Taki frown.
Maria, this is serious, it's not right.
Who cares! We're married now by what's that called? Time served -Maria is still laughing.
People will find out. get married.
We have to
(teasing) Yeah Dad, you better get married, you're starting to look old. The room is quiet. Too soon?
Ian discreetly shakes his head.
Toula leans back.
The Priest says he can do it, we can go today.
Maria stops laughing.
Sounds good. Let's go.
I'm not ready to get married -Ian pivots back.
We're married already!
Then why do we need to see the Priest?!
Gus looks confused.
What's going on?
Maria gets up, paces. Then... blurts:
I want you to propose. What?!
I did.
No, not really. You didn't say it right and it's been bothering me for 50 years.
The family exchanges looks.
What did I say?
You don't remember?
It was a long time ago!
You said, "I'm going to America, you coming or not?" All the women wince.
The men nod, sounds good.
It's direct./What's wrong with it?/ Better than me.
Ilaria swats Nick.
Yeah, you said, "I want to put a baby in you."
I thought it was romantic.
And that was the last time you tried to be romantic. Nobody warns you when you get married, the romance is gone, just like that!
That lands on Toula and Ian. They can't look at each other. Aunt Voula sees this reaction between them, takes it in.
Can we focus on my parents here?
Ma, what's wrong?
Maria is upset, trying to decipher her feelings.
I don't know. I just don't want to get married today.
Disconsolate, Maria goes upstairs.
Toula, do something.
Toula starts to follow Maria. Aunt Voula stops Toula.
I'll talk to her. Everybody out.
Everyone avoids eye contact with Gus and quickly disperses.
INT. MILLER MASTER BEDROOM - EVENING

Toula hurriedly sorts laundry, knocks over a picture. It's Paris, at four years old. Toula sits on the bed, sighs. Holding a Marshalls bag, Aunt Voula walks in, lays a WHITE LACE DOILY over the back area of an arm chair.
Um, hi Thea.
Just a present from me. did your living room. Ah.
I already
